title: Infection Control & Standard Precautions
subtitle: Basic Nursing Care · preventing transmission on the unit
scenario: Core infection-control principles the psychiatric technician applies with every client, every shift.

steps.q: Which action is the MOST effective way to prevent the spread of infection between clients?

opts.text: Wearing gloves for every client interaction
opts.rat: Gloves help but are not a substitute for hand hygiene and can spread organisms if not changed and combined with handwashing.

opts.text: Performing hand hygiene before and after every client contact
opts.rat: Hand hygiene is the single most effective measure to prevent healthcare-associated infection (CDC/WHO).

opts.text: Wearing a surgical mask throughout the shift
opts.rat: Masks are used for specific droplet/airborne situations, not routine prevention of contact spread.

opts.text: Placing every client in a private room
opts.rat: Private rooms are used for specific transmission-based precautions, not as a general measure.

steps.q: When are alcohol-based hand rubs NOT adequate, requiring soap and water instead?

opts.rat: Alcohol rub is appropriate here if hands are not visibly soiled.

opts.text: After removing gloves with no visible soil
opts.rat: Alcohol rub is acceptable when hands are not visibly soiled.

opts.text: When hands are visibly soiled or the client has C. difficile
opts.rat: Alcohol rubs do not remove visible soil and are not sporicidal; wash with soap and water for C. difficile or norovirus.
Alcohol rub is sufficient if hands are clean and not visibly soiled.

steps.q: Standard precautions are used with which clients?

opts.text: Only clients with a known infection
opts.rat: Limiting to known infections misses undiagnosed carriers — the reason standard precautions exist.
Isolation adds transmission-based precautions on TOP of standard precautions.
Standard precautions apply to every client because infection status is often unknown.
Appearance does not rule out a transmissible organism.

steps.q: A client is admitted with active pulmonary tuberculosis. Which precaution is required?

opts.text: Contact precautions with gown and gloves
opts.rat: Contact precautions are for organisms spread by touch, not airborne TB.

opts.text: Droplet precautions with a surgical mask
opts.rat: TB spreads by small airborne particles that a surgical mask does not filter.

opts.text: Airborne precautions with an N95 respirator and a negative-pressure room
opts.rat: TB is airborne; it requires an N95/fit-tested respirator and an airborne-infection isolation (negative-pressure) room.
TB requires airborne precautions in addition to standard precautions.

steps.q: What is the correct order for REMOVING (doffing) personal protective equipment?

opts.rat: This risks contaminating the hands and face; gloves (most contaminated) come off first.
CDC doffing sequence removes the most contaminated item (gloves) first and the respirator last, followed by hand hygiene.
Removing the gown before gloves contaminates the hands.
Gloves should be removed first as the most contaminated item.

steps.q: Which situation calls for CONTACT precautions?

opts.rat: Influenza is droplet spread, calling for droplet precautions.

opts.text: A client with a draining MRSA wound
opts.rat: MRSA and other multidrug-resistant organisms spread by contact; use gown and gloves.
Measles is airborne, requiring airborne precautions.
Pertussis is droplet spread, requiring droplet precautions.

steps.q: A client has seasonal influenza. Which PPE is indicated when working within 3 feet of the client?

opts.rat: N95 is for airborne pathogens; influenza is spread by droplets.
Droplet precautions for influenza call for a surgical mask when close to the client.
Respiratory protection, not just a gown, is the priority for droplet spread.
Droplet precautions require a mask when near the client.

steps.q: Immediately after removing and discarding gloves, the technician should:

opts.text: Apply a new pair of gloves for the next task
opts.rat: Hand hygiene must occur first; gloves can have unseen tears and hands can be contaminated during removal.
Hands can become contaminated during glove removal, so hand hygiene is performed immediately after doffing gloves.

opts.text: Document the care provided, then wash hands
opts.rat: Hand hygiene comes first to avoid contaminating charts and surfaces.

opts.text: Touch only clean surfaces until the next client
opts.rat: Hand hygiene, not avoidance, is the standard after glove removal.
